diagrams.title: Process State Transition Diagram
diagrams.description: A finite state machine diagram showing the five classic states of a process (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated) and the transitions between them. This helps students understand the complete lifecycle of a process in an operating system.
elements: Arrow from Ready to Running (scheduler dispatch)
Arrow from Running to Waiting (I/O or event wait)
Arrow from Waiting to Ready (I/O or event complete)
diagrams.purpose: Explain the fundamental lifecycle of a process and how it moves between different states in the OS

diagrams.title: Scheduling Queues Visualization
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing how processes are organized in different queues: the Job queue, Ready queue, and various I/O device queues. The CPU is shown at the center with processes flowing in and out.
elements: Ready queue (rectangle in center-top, showing FIFO structure)
Process control blocks (small rectangles representing processes)
Arrows showing process flow: Job→Ready→CPU, CPU→I/O queues, I/O queues→Ready
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how the OS scheduler manages multiple processes using different queues and the role of various scheduling levels

diagrams.title: Scheduling Algorithms Gantt Chart Comparison
diagrams.description: Three horizontal timeline charts (Gantt charts) comparing FCFS, SJF (Non-preemptive), and Round Robin scheduling algorithms using the same set of processes with arrival times and burst times.
elements: Three horizontal timeline axes labeled 'FCFS', 'SJF', 'Round Robin'
Process bars in different colors (P1=blue, P2=green, P3=red, P4=orange)
Context switches marked with vertical dashed lines in Round Robin
Waiting time and turnaround time annotations below each chart
Sample data: P1(BT=8, AT=0), P2(BT=4, AT=1), P3(BT=2, AT=2), P4(BT=6, AT=3)
diagrams.purpose: Compare how different scheduling algorithms affect process execution order, waiting time, and turnaround time visually
